aims: This course provides an introduction to the cultural, economic, and global contexts that define the environment for international business. The course seeks to provide you with theoretical and practical knowledge of the issues relevant to international business that will help you address IB situations during your professional career. The course will examine different topics of international business such globalization; differences in culture and ethical issues; trade and investments; regional integration (NAFTA/USMCA and the European Union); international strategy; challenges in supply chain, human resources, marketing and finance functions; international entrepreneurship and social business; and business in emerging markets.
course_content: Block 1: Globalization In the first block of the class we will focus on understanding the process of globalization and how it affects the operations of multinational corporations (MNCs). In particular, we clarify what we understand by “globalization”. We identify the main drivers of this global process, describe the shifting nature of the global economy, explain the main arguments in the debate over the impact of globalization and describe how the process of globalization is creating opportunities and challenges for MNCs. Further, we explore how the political, economic and legal systems of countries differ and how can this impact international business. Introduction Globalization National differences in political economy Political economy and economic development Block 2: Cultural differences and ethical issues in international business In the second block of the class we study how differences in culture can influence the operations of MNCs when venturing abroad. We explain what is meant by the culture of a society (“the collective programming of the mind”) and identify the business implications of differences in culture. Further, we identify the most pressing ethical issues that are faced by international businesses. We define what an ethical dilemma is, identify the causes of unethical behavior by managers, and describe different philosophical approaches to ethics in international business. Ethics in International Business Differences in culture Workshop: Measuring cross-cultural differences Block 3: International trade (exports/imports), Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), regional integration (NAFTA, European Union and others) In the third block of the class we try to understand why nations trade with each other. We describe several theories that attempt to explain why trade flows between nations. We study arguments both in favor and against international trade. Further, we identify the advantages and disadvantages of regional integration, such as NAFTA and the European Union. Finally, we explore the processes that firms have to follow in order to export and import in the international marketplace. International trade theory The political economy of international trade Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) Regional economic integration, NAFTA and the European Union and others Exporting, importing and countertrade Block 4: The foreign exchange market, international monetary system and the global capital market In this block of the course we focus on understanding the global monetary system. We describe the functions of the foreign exchange market, understand what is meant by spot and forward rates, recognize the effect of foreign exchange risk on the activities of the firm and explain how firms try to minimize such risk. Further, we describe the development of the international monetary system and understand how a global capital market is beneficial for both borrowers and lenders. The foreign exchange market The global capital market Workshop: FOREX trading practice session with OANDA The international monetary system MIDTERM EXAMINATION Midterm review Block 5: The challenges of firm multinationality: Strategy, organization, entry strategies, exporting. We start this block with a review of the main theories and frameworks used in the field of business administration. We recognize how firms can profit by expanding globally, and how they must adapt their strategy and organization to maximize this goal. We understand how pressures for cost reductions and pressures for local responsiveness influence strategic choice. We identify the different strategies for competing globally, and how firms can use different entry modes to best take advantage of opportunities abroad. Finally, we explain the promises and risks associated with exporting. Special session: Review of main frameworks in business administration (Porter’s Five Forces, VRIO, Porter’s Diamond, etc.) The strategy of international business The organization of international business Entry strategy and strategic alliances Block 6: International business functions: Value chain, marketing, human resources and finance Building up on the last block, in this part of the course we study the specific challenges that the most important functional areas of the firm face when operating across borders. We explain why production and logistics decisions are of central importance to many MNCs, describe how core marketing functions are affected by internationalization, explain the strategic role of human resource management in the international business and discuss the how operating in different nations impacts finance decisions. Global production, outsourcing and logistics Global marketing and R&D Global human resource management Accounting and Finance in the International Business Block 7: Advanced session: Business in Emerging Markets In this block we study the opportunities and challenges of operating in emerging markets. These markets are characterized by “institutional voids” that make business more difficult, but also more profitable for firms that know how to navigate in these countries. Business in emerging markets Block 7: Group presentations and optional Course wrap-up / Q&A session In this block all the groups will present their final projects. In an additional, optional session, I will answer all remaining questions that you might still have before the final exam. Group presentations (selected at random) Group presentations (selected at random) Group presentations (selected at random) Course wrap-up and Q&A session
learning_outcomes: 1. Understand the different challenges business face when operating in an international environment; 2. Examine various cultural, political, and economic issues that impact international business; 3. Develop an understanding of the concepts and theories that explain international markets; 4. Understand how firms respond to opportunities and risks in the international business environment; 5. Enhance your awareness of how current world events may impact on firms and countries. 6. Understand the particular challenges and opportunities of international entrepreneurship and social businesses. 7. Recognize the challenges of operating in weak institutional environments, such as in emerging markets.
assessment_methods: Class Participation 20% Midterm 20% Final Project 20% Final Exam 40% Class Participation 20%: Attendance, level of preparation, ability to answer questions, and involvement in discussions and other class activities. Midterm 20%: Partial exam near the middle of the course (see dates below for exact day). Includes everything covered before the date of the midterm. Final Project 20%: This team project (groups of 5 students) will require students to use all the topics studied through the course, and apply them in a practical and professional way. The goal of the project is to design an international business plan and strategy for a chosen company in a given country. You are free to choose the company, but do it wisely (ask yourself: What can we learn from this company?). The project should cover everything from basic company and industry information and analysis; country analysis; internationalization strategy; marketing, finance, operations, and HR issues; risks and opportunities; future projections, among others. Final Exam 40%: Date of final examination is provided by the university and cannot be changed under any circumstance. Includes all materials covered throughout the course, including those covered in the midterm. Needs to be approved to obtain a grade for the course. Important to know: Attendance to 80% of the classes is required to receive a grade for the course. *In practice, this means that you are allowed to miss a maximum of 6 sessions. Final exam needs to be approved with a grade above 6.0 to be able to receive a grade for the course.

aims: This course is centered on transnational corporations. The organizational challenges they face and the managerial implications implicit in these challenges. Students should be provided with a basic understanding of how to conduct business through different borders and cultures. The managerial challenges and opportunities that multinationals face, the formulation and assessment of different entry strategies, organizational structures and control issues, and finally, how to assess leadership and motivation issues within a global context in international business career tracks.

aims: Business Strategy I is an introductory course where both the foundations and the new trends of strategic management are studied. In addition, the industry where the organization operates is analyzed at the macro level. Internally, we focus on its resources and capabilities in order to identify and study the different sources of competitive advantage. The mission of this course is to introduce the basic concepts of administration and strategy in order to develop the student’s the ability to identify and critically analyze the elements that underlie the formulation of strategies in various types of organizations. This course will encourage students to begin to develop a critical and informed approach to different management situations that will serve as the basis for their professional development as an actuary, administrator, accountant, financial director or business engineer. At the end of the course, the student will be able to identify and use the tools learned to generate competitive strategies through the use of the theories and models used throughout the course.
course_content: Work plan The course is divided into four main sections. Section I. Introduction, definition of strategy and competitive advantage. It is imperative to understand the importance of establishing a strategy for the firm to generate value and maintain it. In this context we will understand the importance of establishing the mission, vision, values of the business and, of course, the components of the strategy. Section II. Tools for the fundamental analysis of the industry and the firm. In this section we will review classic tools that help to understand the dynamics of the industry from abroad and the elements that from within the firm can generate the desired competitive strategy. Section III. Generation of generic strategies. Once the dynamics of the industry and the forces within the firm are clear, it is possible to generate the corresponding generic strategies to obtain a competitive advantage. Section IV. Diverse topics in strategy. In this section we will review in a general way some of the issues that are relevant for strategic decision making. Section I. Introduction, definition of strategy and competitive advantage 1. Introduction to the course 2. Introduction to strategy and the strategic management process 3. The concept of strategy I 4. The concept of strategy II 5. Case study I 6. Case study II 7. Additional activities Section II. Tools for the fundamental analysis of the firm and the industry 8. Industry analysis I 9. Industry analysis II 10. Case study 11. Internal analysis 12. Case study 13. SWOT analysis 14. SWOT in practice 15. Review session for midterm Midterm exam (date to be determined) 16. Discussion of midterm results Section III. Generic strategies and corporate strategies 17. Generic strategies 18. Cost leadership 19. Product diversification 20. Case studies 21. Blue Ocean Strategy 22. Corporate strategies. Vertical and horizontal integration 23. Analysis of corporate strategies: case studies by groups Section IV. Select topics in strategy 24. Types of organization 25. Case study 26. International strategies 27. Case study 28. Strategic alliances, mergers and acquisitions 29. Student presentations 30. Student presentations 31. Course recap
learning_outcomes: Get to know the different schools of thought and the roles of the administrator in order to grasp the conceptual frameworks of this discipline and its trends. Characterize the notion of strategy so that the student understands the different ways in which a company can interact with its environment taking advantage of access to resources and internal capabilities. Develop analytical, critical and proactive skills through study and research based on practical cases. Provide students with criteria to evaluate, through conceptual frameworks, analysis models and other tools, diverse situations and decisions that involve variables at the external level (industry) and internally (organization). Use skills to simulate the behavior of a company. Develop argumentative and writing skills to better communicate ideas.
assessment_methods: Controls (pop quizzes): They will be applied throughout the course without prior notice and their intention is to review the understanding of the topics and provide feedback to students on their performance. Group work: At the beginning of the course teams of 3 or 4 people will be formed and students will work throughout the semester on various activities. Cases: Throughout the course several cases elaborated by professors of ITAM or Harvard cases will be used. These are materials to develop discussion and writing skills for students in such a way that it is necessary to prepare the corresponding case according to the questions assigned by the instructor. For the discussion, all students must prepare the case and individually submit a brief report of it. This is classified as delivered or not delivered at the end of the discussion. For purposes of the discussion, the teams will be assigned one of the cases and the team will lead the corresponding discussion. The team in question will have an evaluation according to the rubric given by the instructor. Participation: Participation will be assessed throughout the semester and the evaluation will depend on: the student's commitment to the class (50%), active participation (50%). Midterm exam covering the first part of the course (date to be assigned). Final exam at the end of the course is cumulative, and on the date assigned by the department. Passing the final exam is essential to pass the course. In the event that the student is found to be committing a fraudulent practice, the course will be classified as not accredited (N.A. or no credit NC) and the corresponding notification will be included in the student's file (Article 29 of the Student Rulebook). Assistance and participation are essential to be able to pass the course. To qualify for the final exam the student must have a minimum attendance of 80%; that is, not miss more than six sessions throughout the semester. The student is responsible for managing their absences. Only medical notes for absences issued by the school’s registrar are accepted. The cell phone policy is that they can be used, but with discretion. The student that abuses the use of cell phones in class will be sanctioned with participation. Cell phones, tablets and laptops are allowed to be online in silent mode for educational use only (for example, dictionary, Google, online resources). Please, NO texting, NO listening to music, NO browsing the web. Electronic devices are NOT allowed during exams, except for a simple calculator (if necessary). Evaluation Controls (pop quizzes) 20% Group work 05% Participation 10% Midterm exam 25% Final exam 25% Essay/Project 15%

aims: This course provides an introduction to strategic thinking in the context of business. The objective of the course is to develop tools useful to making decisions in situations involving counterfactual scenarios. Counterfactual scenarios are situations that have never been observed in the past. Such situations arise due to technological change such as the introduction of the smartphone, demographic change such as the increase in life expectancy, policy change such as international trade liberalization, a competitor acquiring a patent, a merger of two competitors or suppliers, etc. The core ideas developed in this course revolve around the following themes: consumer demand and firm competitive advantage, dynamic vs. static decisions, decision-making under uncertainty, applied game theory, balanced score cards, ethics and corporate social responsibility. Considerable time will be spend on the applied game theory part of the course in which we will analyze situations where the choices that we make affect others and the choices others make that affect us. Such situations are known as games. We will discuss real-world examples and analyze situations that arise frequently in business settings. Regarding games, the focus of the course will be on the application of game-theoretic concepts, not mathematical proofs.
course_content: 1 Basic economic concepts Definition of the generic decision problem and motivation for the need of theory and data in decision-making, competitive advantage, sustaining competitive advantage, consumer demand, strategic positioning. 2 Co-opetition The value net and the importance of complementors. 3 Basic game-theoretic concepts Players, payoffs, strategies, equilibrium, categorization of games (simultaneous, dynamic), dominance and iterated dominance, Nash equilibrium, backwards induction. 4 Uncertainty and scenario planning Decision making under uncertainty, construction of scenarios, option value, generic tactics in uncertain situations. Midterm Exam The exact date of the midterm will be determined later on in the semester. The material covered in the exam will become evident as the time approaches. 5 Game-theoretic applications Commitment, value added, changing the game in your favor. 6 Balanced Score Cards The tool of balanced score cards for internal organization and planning. 7 Business Ethics Ethical systems and business ethics. 8 Corporate Social Responsibility Corporate social responsibility and competitive advantage.
learning_outcomes: After successfully completing this course students should be able to - Identify counterfactual scenarios - Understand the importance of consumer heterogeneity for firm profitability - Separate dynamic from static decisions. - Use the concept of equilibrium to construct consistent scenarios. - Solve for the equilibria of a game using the following equilibrium concepts: Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies, Nash equilibrium, Backwards induction, Subgame-perfect equilibrium. - Demonstrate an understanding of the importance of corporate social responsibility and ethics for firm performance.
assessment_methods: Participation: Even though attendance is not mandatory to pass the course, in-class participation constitutes 10% of the final grade. I try to reward effort and creativity through participation. Tests: Short tests might be administered in the beginning of each lecture with or without prior notice. Team work: You should form teams of five people by the end of the second week of classes. It is important to learn early on to work with others and team assignments serve this purpose. Teams will be assigned homework and in-class presentations. Term paper/Essay: Term papers (sort essays) are due towards the end of the semester. Each student is required to analyze a real-world business case using some of the tools developed in class. Evaluation of Performance and Grading Throughout the semester students will have the opportunity to earn up to 10 points. aims: The Circular Economy seeks to redefine growth and development, decoupling it from finite resource consumption and eliminating waste from the system. Underpinned by a transition to renewable energy and materials, it generates economic, social and environmental capital based on three principles: i) eliminate waste and pollution; ii) circulate products and materials at their highest value for longer; and iii) regenerate nature. Circularity for a Sustainable Future seeks to promote sustainable and inclusive entrepreneurship initiatives among students. It will familiarize them with the Circular Economy fundamentals: its principles, key concepts and most common tools and business models, combined with Human-Centered Design principles and methodology to equip students with the tools necessary to design and implement circular and inclusive projects aimed at tackling the most pressing social, environmental and economic problems.
course_content: 1: Sustainability and the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development Schools of thought and theory, and basic concepts Systems, futures and design thinking 2030 Agenda: background and structure, SDGs, mainstreaming and evaluation 2: The Fourth Industrial Revolution and Introduction to the Circular Economy 4.0 technologies, technology adoption curve and innovation matrix Material flows and life-cycle analysis Strategies and business models Social considerations of technological disruption 3: Introduction to Human-Centered Design and the Circular Business Canvas HCD: Background, principles and mindset HCD: Process stages CBC: what it is and how it differs from other tools 4: Diversity, Equity and Inclusion What it is and why it’s a pillar of sustainability Frameworks and debates Intersectionality 5: Group presentation #1 Chosen sector and justification Legal and institutional framework Problems identified and why it’s important to solve them 6: Plastics Brief history and typology of plastics Alternatives and treatments INC on Plastic Pollution and the New Plastics Economy 7: Textiles Why textiles? Alternatives and treatments What’s already under way 8: Food and agriculture SDG#2 interlinkages and WFE-CC Nexus Alternatives 9: Construction and circular cities The built environment What is a circular city Frameworks and evaluation 10: Group presentation #2 Target customer group Chosen products or services and possible strategies 11: Partial exam Classes 1-10. 12: Material flows, life-cycle analysis and life-cycle redefinition Butterfly diagram Life-cycle analysis fundamentals CBC: Customer segments, value proposition and channels 13. Circular strategies and business models 10 Rs Design, optimal use, value recovery, and circular support models CBC: Customer relationships, revenue streams and key resources 14: Evaluating circularity Linear vs circular evaluation Circular evaluation frameworks CBC: Key activities, key partners and costs 15: Group presentation #3 Business models Expected challenges and opportunities 16: Final exam Classes 1-15. 17: Conclusion General feedback on final reports Course highlights and takeaways Suggestions on course content and format
learning_outcomes: At the end of the course, students will be able to: 1. Think critically, systemically, and circularly; 2. Fluently speak the Circular Economy language; and 3. Identify, design and value circular solutions in any sector, from an economic, social, and environmental point of view.
assessment_methods: The course will be taught mainly through a combination of the lecture, discussion, and assignment-based methods. In addition to quizzes and exams, students will work on four types of complementary activities throughout the course: 1. Prior to each class: reading of relevant reports and success stories; 2. During each class: discussion and short exercises; 3. Throughout the course, individually: research and formulation of a specific project for the chosen sector; and 4. Throughout the course, in a group: idea exchange and comparison of differences and similarities (to be shared with the class in the form of a sectorial presentation). Class participation Quality of student interventions: questions, comments, examples, etc. 5% Quizzes Five quizzes to verify the understanding of basic concepts. 10% Group presentations Three group presentations summarizing sectoral needs, differences and similarities between projects/ programs, and common challenges and opportunities identified. 15% Partial exam One exam with three short essay questions, covering the topics discussed in classes 1 to 10. 15% Individual partial deliveries In preparation for the final project, students will submit two partial projects of two pages each: 1. General context of the sector and location they have chosen and the problem they want to tackle, highlighting the importance of solving it and the approach they’d like to work from (public, private or civil society) and why; and 2. Profile of their project or program, highlighting the business/ operational model, product or service, and target customer/ beneficiary group. 15% Final exam One exam with multiple choice, true-false and completion questions, and one essay question, covering the topics discussed in classes 1 to 14. 15% Final report Detailed report of the chosen sector and location, legal and institutional framework within which the proposed project or program would be operating, description of the problem or need to address, implementation approach, business/ operational model, product/ service, target customer/ beneficiary group, expected challenges and opportunities, and basic financial model and evaluation framework. 25%

aims: The study of organizational behavior considers the employee as an individual, the team as a micro-social group, and the company as a system and society. It also studies the relationships between these. The goal is to discover better management practices that recognize and harness the human aspect—both psychological and cultural—in the functioning of a company. A good manager is a good psychologist and a good sociologist, and is fully aware that a company’s human resources have emotions, perceptions, personalities, abilities, knowledge, potentials, limitations, interests, bothers, prejudices, customs, beliefs, habits, and traditions.
course_content: The study of organizational behavior considers the employee as an individual, the team as a micro-social group, and the company as a system and society. It also studies the relationships between these. The goal is to discover better management practices that recognize and harness the human aspect—both psychological and cultural—in the functioning of a company. A good manager is a good psychologist and a good sociologist, and is fully aware that a company’s human resources have emotions, perceptions, personalities, abilities, knowledge, potentials, limitations, interests, bothers, prejudices, customs, beliefs, habits, and traditions. Cases We will do 3 or 4 cases during the course. You will do them in randomly assigned teams of 5 students each. You will analyze and solve a case, and prepare a PowerPoint to present your solution. It must be well-practiced, because you will have only 8 minutes to do your presentation. If you don’t finish on time, you lose points.
learning_outcomes: To familiarize ourselves with various relevant theories that address human behavior in the context of company performance. Establish the difference between values, perception attitude, personality, and behavior. Distinguish the various motivation theories and understand the effect of different policies on the average performance of employees. To familiarize ourselves with the benefits, as well as the difficulties and challenges, that group behavior and work teams present. To reason about possible solutions to the most common problems. To learn to differentiate situations that require an individual vs. a team effort. To identify obstacles inherent in the decisión-making process, individually and in groups. To learn how to utilize the library databases for academic research. To build, based on library research, academic-style reports that properly cite sources from a bibliographic, empirical, logical, and ethical point of view. This includes properly applying the AoM style of referencing, which is approved for thesis work. To understand better the managerial and labor culture in Mexico, and the application of management theories to the Mexican context. To distinguish and apply various leadership theories to concrete cases. To explain the role played by power and politics in the implantation of organizational initiatives. To identify factors critical to the success of organizationa change. To identify the advantages and disadvantages of organizational culture.
assessment_methods: Question sets You will receive question sets on the assigned readings. These question sets are the basis for class discussion. There will be a short quiz (two questions, multiple-choice) at the beginning of each class in which a question set is due. The quizzes will be designed so that only people who actually did the homework will be able to answer them. Being two-question quizzes, the only possible grades will be 0, 50, and 100. You must do these question sets in teams of 3 or 4 students. The work must not be divided. You are to meet together, in real time (physically or virtually), to discuss the answers to each and every question. I want you to have a debate. No more than two or three short paragraphs per answer. The better you do these question sets, the better you are preparing for the multiple-choice partial exam, and for the multiple-choice final exam. Relationship between question sets and participation Your participation in class will reflect whether you did have a debate when doing the question sets. Cases We will do 3 or 4 cases during the course. You will do them in randomly assigned teams of 5 students each. You will analyze and solve a case, and prepare a PowerPoint to present your solution. Course evaluation 40% Cases plus partials 30% Class participation 10% Simulations 20% Final Exam (you must pass it in order to pass the course) NOTE: You require 80% assistance IN CLASS in order to keep your right to the final exam (and therefore to pass the course). Whoever accumulates 6 absences will be notified of his/her IMMEDIATE PERIL. The seventh absence will mean DOOM: can’t do the final; can’t pass. Whoever arrives 15 minutes late or more, will be marked as “tardy.” Two of these are the same as an absence, and will count towards your 6. Every two absences will shave 10 points (over 100) from your participation grade.

aims: Your success in the working world depends in a large part on your ability to communicate ideas and arguments effectively. This class will help students to develop the communication skills necessary for successful business communication, focusing primarily on their public speaking and presentation skills. Developing these managerial skills will help students to achieve success in their careers at ITAM and beyond. Students will develop these analytical and communication skills through written assignments, in-class discussion, individual and group presentations, and role-playing exercises. In addition, this course will pay particular attention to preparing ITAM students for the challenges they will face in the English-speaking business world.
course_content: Introduction to Communication Strategies: Introduction to the Course Expectations for written work Presentation Activity Explain presentation analysis assignment Communicator Strategy Audience Strategy Introduction to First Individual Presentation Message Strategy Culture Strategy Form groups for class group work Sign up for 1st Individual Presentation Structure and Persuasion of Arguments: Theory and Practice The Pyramid Principle, Barbara Minto Building the Pyramid Introductions: Situation, Complication, Question Explanation of group projects Teamwork exercise Minto In-Class Presentations Speaking: Verbal Structure Speaking: Non-Verbal Skills Discussion of “Learning Charisma” Using transitions while speaking Transition exercise Evaluated Group Presentation: “Harnessing the Science of Persuasion” Feedback on Group Presentations Individual Presentations Speaking: Using Visual Aids Group Presentation Women and leadership: discussion and in-class debate Proposals for change: group presentations Giving feedback Authentic Leadership Introduction Exercise Final Group Project: 1st Presentation Entering the Workplace and Communication in the Workplace: CV, Resumes, and cover letters Social media, privacy, and image control CV and Resumes Job Interviews Mock Job Interviews Discussion and presentation: “Sunk in Sacramento” Final Presentations and Conclusions: Final Group Project: Final Presentations Feedback on Final Group Presentations Final Individual Presentations Feedback on Final Individual Presentations and Final Thoughts
learning_outcomes: By the end of the course, students will be able to: analyze their own methods of communication and generate personal strategies for strengthening those communication skills deliver a successful oral presentation in English analyze the elements of an oral presentation and use this analysis to give specific, constructive feedback to peers develop analytical and critical skills and demonstrate these skills during class discussion, written work, oral presentations, and exams identify the elements of successful self-presentation and to demonstrate this knowledge by presenting themselves professionally and coherently in a cv, cover letter, and job interview work effectively in peer groups to achieve course goals
assessment_methods: Your final grade will be comprised of the following elements: Participation and Homework 25 % Presentations 65 % Individual Presentations: 30% Group presentations: 35% Final Exam 10 % Failure of any portion of the grade (i.e. homework and written assignments, first individual presentation, etc.) will lower your grade and may result in failing the course. If you fail the final exam, you will fail the course. All students must complete the group diary (assignment to be explained in class) in order to pass the class. You must pass the final exam to pass the course. The final exam will be cumulative. Additionally, you must attend at least 80% of the class sessions in order to be able to take the final exam. Because much of the learning of this class takes place during class discussion, class attendance and active participation—contributing as both a listener and a discussant—is mandatory. In-class work and assignments cannot be made up, and students are responsible for obtaining materials and information distributed in class. Absence during an exam or presentation will result in a failing grade. Your attendance and participation grade will evaluate your active participation in class, your attendance, and your commitment to the class (arriving on time, participating actively, completing assignments, communicating with the professor in a responsible matter, and otherwise being an engaged and responsible student). After each presentation, you will email me a self-evaluation of at least 1-2 paragraphs analysing qualitatively your presentation. Additionally, you will be asked to give informal and formal feedback to classmates as part of the same process.

aims: The main objective of the course is to is to introduce the student to one of the most important financial areas in the last years: capital markets. The course starts with a description of financial assets and market. Also, a review of statistical concepts to be used along the course is made. Next, the course introduces the Modern Portfolio Theory beginning with concepts like preference and risk aversion. A mean-variance model is developed with a special emphasis on the risk-return relation, which is a basic precept for any financial decision, and on the benefits of diversification. Next,the course presents Asset Pricing Models. It presents the Capital Asset Pricing Model, discussing its advantages and critiques. Next the Arbitrage Pricing Theory is presented. This section is wrapped up with an analysis of efficient market hypothesis. The last section of the course seeks to impart knowledge on share analysis. This includes delving into share characteristics and share valuation models (technical analysis, relative valuation and cash flow valuation). A review of financial statement is also done.

Investment portfolio Modern Theory Risk and risk aversion Distributing funds between risky and non-risky assets Optimal portfolio selection III. Capital Asset Pricing Model Capital Asset Pricing Model Index Model Arbitrage Pricing Theory Market Efficiency IV. Shares analysis Valuation by multiples Valuation by Cash Flow

aims: Fundamentals of Finance is an introductory course, which aims to study the general Principles in Finance and give students a complete overview of Finance. The basis of specialized courses will be established in this course. The course of Fundamentals of Finance covers all the Finance subthemes under the same conceptual framework. It is expected that students acquire knowledge about the Principles of Corporate Finance, the Functioning of Financial Markets, Investments, and Modern Valuation Techniques for Fixed Income Securities, Equities and Derivatives. The study of Finance refers to the allocation of scarce resources over time under uncertainty conditions. Considering this approach, this course is oriented to provide students the knowledge of the three related pillars of finance that support financial theory, which are interrelated: a) Optimization over time (exchange relations over time) b) Valuation of financial securities (bond and stocks) c) Corporate Finance In relation to the course content, the first part pretends that students understand the mechanism through which individuals select among different alternatives with uncertain payments, specifically refers to consumption and investment decisions. Also, in this part of the course, it is highlighted the importance of a basic financial precept “One dollar today values more than one dollar tomorrow”, traditionally known as Time Value of Money. This same concept will be used for valuing bonds and stocks. Additionally, the term structure of interest rates are going to be studied, because is one of the relevance issues of the Fixed Income Market. In a second stage, the course will focus on the study of the different techniques used for valuing investment projects under conditions of certainty and uncertainty, analyzing the advantages and disadvantages of each one. There will be an important emphasis in the building of cash flows, and also in the calculation of the Weighted Average Cost of Capital. In the third stage, the course will be focused on the study of the principles of Portfolio Investment Theory, in particular in the concepts of risk preference and risk aversion. The mean variance model will be studied, specially highlighting the relation between risk and return, which is a basic precept for any financial decision considering the benefits of diversification. Finally, students will study the basic concepts about Derivatives. The main objective is that students will get related with the terminology used in Derivatives, and acquire the knowledge and understanding of the characteristics of such instruments.
course_content: I. Introduction to Finance A. Goals and Governance of the Firm 1. Goals in Corporate Finance 2. Goals of the Corporation 3. The Role of the Financial Manager B. Financial System 1. Money and Capital Markets 2. Primary and Secondary Markets 3. Fixed Income Securities and Variable Income Securities C. Code of Ethics in Finance Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct II. Consumption and Investment Decisions A. Consumption and Investment Decisions B. Relationship between Consumption and Investment Decisions and Financial Markets C. The Fisher Separation Theorem D. Analysis of Investment Projects III. Financial Mathematics A. Time Value of Money. Present Value and Future Value. B. Simple Interest Rate and Compound Interest Rate. Nominal Interest Rate, Effective Interest Rate and Equivalent Interest Rates. Nominal and Real Rates of Interest. C. Discount Interest Rates and Yield Rates. D. Annuities and Perpetuities with and without growing. E. Amortization Schedules 1. Constant Payments 2. Constant Principal Repaid 3. Adhoc Schemes IV. Valuation Models A. Principles of Assets Valuation B. Valuing Bonds 1. Basic Concepts and Classification 2. Bond Pricing 3. Yield Measures 4. Duration C. Term Structure of Interest Rates D. The Value of Common Stocks 1. Basic Concepts and Classification 2. Stock Pricing and Multiples V. Valuation of Investment Projects A. Introduction to Valuation of Investment Projects 1. The Free Cash Flow 2. Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) 2.1 Determinants of Debt Cost 2.2 Determinants of Capital Cost 3. Capital Structure and Leverage B. Investment Criteria 1. Payback Period 2. Discounted Payback Period 3. Net Present Value 4. Internal rate of Return (IRR) 5. Profitability Index C. Project Analysis under Uncertainty 1. Different Life Projects 2. Optimum Time for Replacement 3. Decision Trees 4. Sensitivity Analysis, Scenario Analysis and Beak-Even point VI. Valuing Assets A. Portfolio Theory B. Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) C. Market Efficiency D. Introduction to Derivatives 1. Futures 2. Options

During the semester the teacher will assign homework with a specific delivery date, the teacher won’t receive any homework in other date than that initially established. All the exams will be applied in a computer room, and student could use a spreadsheet or a financial calculator to solve them. Each week, there will be a lab class, of 1.30 hour duration, which will be taught by a student of the Financial Management degree whom passed this course with an excellent score. aims: The course focuses on financial theory used in investment decision making. For this purpose, it starts with an introduction to the function of financial instruments and markets. Likewise, statistical concepts are presented as well as quantitative aspects necessary to implement the valuation of the financial instruments and models that are developed throughout the second part of the course. The second part of the study centers on the techniques and implications of an efficient diversification. It is brought to attention the effect of efficient diversification on appropriate measures of risks and performance. Next, equilibrium valuation methods for financial actives are presented; specifically the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) and finally, the new Factor Theory or Factor Investing. The third part of the course consists on the analysis of fixed and variable income markets; the instruments that quote in them, how they are valuated and the risk measures that are applied. Finally, an introduction to derivative markets and relevant measures to measure portfolio performance are presented.
course_content: I The Microstructure of Financial Markets I.1 The surroundings of investments I.2 Financial Markets and Instruments I.3 The Mechanics of Values Quote I.4 Professional Ethics and Standards II The Modern Theory of Investment Portfolios II.1 Risk and Risk Aversion II.2 Portfolio Distribution Between Values with and without Risk II.3 The Selection of Optimal Risk Portfolios II.4 Index Models III The Equilibrium of Capital Markets III.1 The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) III.2 Factor Theory IV Fixed and Variable Income Values IV.1 The Valuation of Fixed Income Values IV.2 Investment in Fixed Income Values IV.3 Industrial and Macroeconomic Analysis IV.4 Action Valuation Models V Introduction to Derivative Instruments V.1 Options Market V.2 Future Market VI Portfolio Administration VI.1 Portfolio Performance Evaluation
learning_outcomes: At the end of the course the student must be able to: Understand the functioning of financial actives and markets. Know the sustentation of modern investment portfolio theory. Acknowledge the principal elements of fixed and variable income markets. Understand the functioning of derivative instruments. Comprehend financial theory routed to construct investment strategies.
assessment_methods: The course will consist on expositions on behalf of the professor, practical exercises by computer, as well as discussion on specific topics. The individual participation is a vital element and could be solicited by the professor. This requires the student preparation before assisting to class, for which is necessary to read chapters and lectures assigned. Likewise, there will be quizzes throughout the course to achieve the objectives.

(AA) Elton J. Edwin and Gruber J. Martin (2014). “Modern Portfolio Theory and Investment Analysis”, 9th ed., USA, John Wiley and Sons, Inc. Danthine Jean-Pierre and Donaldson John B. (2014), “Intermediate Financial Theory”, 3rd ed., Elsevier Academic Press. (DD) CFA Program Curriculum. “Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct” (2014), CFA Institute (CFA) Fabozzi, Frank (2015), “Bond Markets, Analysis and Strategies”, 9th ed., Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, N. J. Hull, John, (2014), “Options, Futures and Other Derivatives”, 9th ed., Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, N. J. The textbook will be complemented con selected articles given throughout the trimester. Additionally, the student is encouraged the daily lecture of national and international newspapers.

aims: In a modern economy, financial markets and financial Institutions play an extremely relevant role in the allocation of resources. Around the world, recurrent financial crises, such as the 2008 financial crisis, demonstrate the crucial role that financial institutions and financial markets play for economic growth, firm performance and households. Such crises also stress the need to understand these institutions and markets that are commonly misunderstood and misrepresented by the media and policy makers alike. Broadly defined the course has two objectives. First, to understand the role of financial markets and financial intermediaries; their basic characteristics, the challenges and opportunities they face in a market economy. Second, to understand the risks financial intermediaries face and the tools to manage such risks exposures.
course_content: Course Overview and Introduction to Financial Intermediation Key ideas: Preferences and risk; risk-aversion; diversification; riskless arbitrage; market efficiency; asymmetry of information: hidden action (agency costs and moral hazard) and hidden information (adverse selection). Financial Institutions: Economic Role, Regulation and Relevance Key ideas: What is financial intermediation? Why should we care about financial intermediation? (i.e. the special role of financial intermediaries (FIs) in the financial system and the functions they provide); understand why various FIs receive special regulatory attention; understand what makes some FIs more special than others; motivate the basics with some highlights of the 2008 financial crisis Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Depositary and non-depositary institutions; commercial banks, savings institutions, credit unions, finance companies, central banks etc. do? What are the similarities and differences among them? Their role in the financial crisis of 2008? Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Security firms; investment banks; mutual funds; hedge funds; balance sheets–assets and liabilities; Russian Default and LCTM Story Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Insurance companies; life insurance and property-casualty insurance; reisurance; underwriting Types and Financial Risks Key ideas: Interest rate risk; credit risk; liquidity risk; foreign exchange risk; sovereign risk; market risk; off-balance-sheet-risk; operational risk; insolvency risk Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Measuring interest rate risk exposure-repricing model; problems with the repricing model; maturity; term structure of interest rate Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Measuring interest rate risk exposure-duration model; problems with the duration gap model; convexity Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Exchange rate risk; interest rate parity; hedging interest rate risk; coverage within and off-balance sheet Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Forward contracts and futures contracts; are they substitutes or they are different; role in hedging interest rate and exchange rate risks Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Options; role in hedging interest rate and exchange rate risks; options vs forwards and futures Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Swaps; interest rate swaps; currency swaps Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Securitization; role in subprime mortgage crisis Course Summary and Overview
learning_outcomes: At the end of this course, students will be able to demonstrate that they understand: The rationale for financial intermediaries, as well as their main functions in a market economy. Identify and evaluate the role symmetric versus asymmetric information plays in the structure and operation of the financial system information. Distinguish the different types of intermediaries and perform financial analysis on them. Understand asset, liability and capital components in the balance sheet of a financial intermediary. Understand how banks earn revenues and profits. Determine the relevant exposures to various types of risks. Understand and use market risk models to manage risk exposure (rates and exchange rate). Understand and use derivative instruments to manage off-Balance Sheet exposures. The basic character of other types of Financial Institutions and roles they play in the economy (including how they are regulated) Technological and other developments impacting the future of Financial Institutions.
assessment_methods: In Class Quiz (15) 15% Homework (3) 15% Midterm Exam (October 9 – In class) 30% Final Exam (Cumulative. Date will be announced later) 40% It is essential to pass the final exam in order to pass the course. No examination will be taken outside the set date and time. If a student is found to have copied on an exam, control or assignment, a zero grade will be considered on this and will be proceeded in accordance with the institute's regulations.
literature: Cornett, Marcia Millon, and Anthony Saunders. Financial institutions management: A risk management approach. McGraw-Hill/Irwin, 2017. Saunders, Anthony, and Marcia Millon Cornett. Financial markets and institutions. McGraw-Hill Education, 2018.

aims: This course covers the foundations of finance with an emphasis on applications that are vital for corporate managers. We will discuss many of the major financial decisions made by corporate managers both within the firm and in their interactions with investors. Essential in these decisions is the process of valuation, which will be an important emphasis of the course. Topics include criteria for making investment decisions, valuation of financial assets and liabilities, relationships between risk and return, capital structure choice, the effective use and valuation of derivative securities, as well as, risk management, and real options.
course_content: Time Value of Money, Present and Future Values, NPV Key ideas: financial statements: basics; time value of money net present value (NPV), and annuity formulas Financial Decision-Making: Free Cash Flow Key ideas: NPV ingredients: (a) cash-flows and (b) discount rates Free cash flows (FCFs): earnings are not FCFs Discount rates: (a) time value and (b) compensation for risk Scenario & Sensitivity Analysis; Application Key ideas: Computing FCFs: scenario and sensitivity analysis Annuity formulas and annuity spreadsheet Effective annual rates (EAR), annual percentage rates (APR) Capital Budgeting: Incremental Cash Flows Key ideas: Computing FCFs: incremental cash-flows, timing of CFs, terminal values, sensitivity analysis The link between risk and the cost of capital Capital budgeting: investment decision rules Key ideas: NPV and the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) IRR as bank savings on a real investment. The limitations of using the IRR rule. Project selection with resource constraints Interest Rates and Bond Valuation: Basics Key ideas: Term structure of interest rates, the yield curve Bond basic characteristics: face value, coupons, maturity, STRIPS, yield to maturity, zero-coupon bonds, etc Evolution of bond prices over time Bond Pricing and No-Arbitrage Key ideas: pricing bonds using no-arbitrage; Interest rate sensitivity of bonds Firm Valuation I Key ideas: Equity cost of capital, capital gains, dividend yield Valuation models: (a) dividends, (b) constant growth model (c) total payout model Firm Valuation II Key ideas: (a) Free cash flow valuation models (b) valuation based on comparable firms ("multiples") Valuing Interco Key ideas: different approaches to valuing a firm Assumptions behind alternative valuation approaches Main: (a) discounted cashflow and (b) multiples Risk, the Cost of Capital, Portfolio Choice, Diversification Key ideas: Expected returns, variance and standard deviation Risk-return tradeoff, covariance, correlation across assets Portfolio risk and diversification Describing portfolios and Optimal Efficient Frontier Key ideas: Optimal investment frontier. Optimal portfolios with short-selling. Portfolios with risk-free investments and leverage Optimal portfolios; Sharpe ratios; Minimum required rate of return Market Pricing of Risk Key ideas: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). CAPM: the market is the tangent portfolio. Capital market line: max expected return per unit of volatility. Security market line: market portfolio as benchmark for other investments. Beta of: (1) a security, (2) an asset are not the same. Using betas to compute the expected return of a security Estimating the Cost of Capital in Practice Key ideas: CAPM implementation: risk-free, market index, betas and risk-premia; unlevered betas; equity & debt betas. Estimating betas using historical returns; Using comparables in estimating betas and the cost of capital; formulas to unlever and re-lever betas Valuing a Firm Key ideas: Valuing a firm in practice: (a) project cashflows, and (b) estimate discount rates. Projecting cash-flows: link between forecasts & operations. Estimating the cost of capital: CAPM; Timing of cash-flows and terminal values Financing Investment: Perfect Capital Markets Key ideas: differences between debt and equity. Debt is senior (less risky) relative to equity: a lower cost does not imply that debt is "cheaper." Difference between unlevered and levered equity Weighted average cost of capital (WACC) & project-specific risk Modigliani-Miller propositions: (a) debt does not affect value (b) WACC does not change with leverage Financing Investment: Fallacies and taxes Key ideas: Capital structure fallacies: (a) dilution, (b) earnings per share. Beyond MM propositions. Corporate income taxes & value: interest tax shields. With corporate taxes, leverage affects the WACC and firm value. Who captures the interest tax shields? Financing Investment: Methods to Value the Leverage Effects Key ideas: Main methods to evaluate the effect of leverage on firm value: (a) WACC and (b) Adjusted present value (APV). Explore why firms are not 100 percent debt financed. Extensions to personal taxes Valuation with Leverage: WACC Key ideas: The impact of the costs of bankruptcy and financial distress (FD) on financing decisions; Picking a target capital structure; Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC); Valuing a going concern; practical Issues in valuation Agency Costs of debt and Adjusted Present Value Key ideas: Agency costs of debt: (a) excessive risk-taking, (b) debt overhang. Agency benefits of leverage. Debt can act as a disciplinary device: force managers to payout. Expanded tradeoff theory: taxes, FD, agency and incentives. WACC and APV valuation Leverage: The Role of Information Key ideas: Capital structure decisions as signals of firm quality The role of information in capital structure decisions & firm value Pecking order hypothesis. Implications of information asymmetry for equity issuance. Capital Structure: Summary and Applications Key ideas: Lessons from the recent financial crisis. Discussion on the capital structure of banks, tax policy. Payout policy: dividends and share repurchases Risk Management: When does RM add value? Key ideas: Rationale for risk-management. Hedging as insurance Forward: contract to buy/sell in the future at a price set today Future contracts: forward contract that is traded on an exchange Financial Options I Key ideas: Options: basic characteristics and payoffs. Call (put) options: right but not the obligation to buy (sell) an asset. Using options for risk-management purposes. Options vs. forwards in risk-management Financial Options II Key ideas: Put-call parity Binomial option pricing: call options Financial Options III Key ideas: Binomial option pricing: multi-period binomial option pricing: put options. Black-Scholes formula Determinants of Option Values Key ideas: Using the Black-Scholes formula in practice Black-Scholes calculator in excel. Valuation of employee stock options (ESOs) Challenges of using Black-Scholes formula for valuing ESOs Implied volatility; Determinants of option prices Dynamic Decision-Making and Real Options Key ideas: Real options: flexible investment projects are like financial options: (a) option to wait, (b) option to expand, (c) option to delay. Implications for investment in practice. Compare a DCF valuation to a real options valuation The value of staging investments Key ideas: the value of staging investments in the pharma and venture capital industries: (a) set milestones in advance (b) postpone substantial investments until information arrives (c) drop projects that do not meet the milestones Corporate Securities as Options and Credit Risk Key ideas: equity can be interpreted as a call option on firm value Equity holders right to default is like a put option on the firm's assets Using Black-Scholes to compute the value of debt and equity Course Overview
assessment_methods: Your course grade will be determined by your class participation and in-class quizzes (15%), performance on each of the two midterm exams (30%), the final exam (40-55%), and completion of recommended homework assignments available through MyFinanceLab (0-15%). The purpose of the weekly MyFinanceLab homework is to keep you up to date with the course material. The amount you complete correctly will determine the weight put on homework in your final grade, up to a maximum of 15%. The remaining weight will be put on the final exam. (For example, if 1500 total homework points are possible, and you complete correctly 1000, homework will count for 10% of your grade, and the final will count for 45%). Because students have unlimited attempts to get assignments right (before each assignment is due), almost all students receive full points on the homework. This means that not doing an assignment puts you at a considerable disadvantage relative to others when the final grades are computed.
literature: Corporate Finance (plus MyFinanceLab), Jonathan Berk and Peter DeMarzo, 3rd ed., Pearson - Prentice Hall, 2013. Guide to Money and Investing, by Virginia B. Morris and Kenneth Morris, Lightbulb Press, 2015 (www.lightbulbpress.com), or any prior edition. Capital Ideas: The Improbable Origins of Modern Wall Street, by Peter L. Bernstein, Wiley, 2009, or any prior edition. A Random Walk Down Wall Street, by Burton G. Malkiel, W. W. Norton & Company, 2015 or any prior edition.

aims: This is an advanced undergraduate course in finance. The course focuses on real world market risk models for equity stocks and equity options. Working on real life models, students develop their intuition in finance matters and learn derivatives pricing, risk management and market model implementation. This course prepares students to work in Risk Management for any financial institution.
course_content: 1. Risk Management and Financial Returns (Ch. 1) 2. Historical Simulation, Value at Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (Ch. 2) Backtesting and Stress Testing 3. Volatility Modeling using daily data (Ch. 3 with special emphasis on Section 4 & Ch. 4) 4. Volatility Modeling using intraday data (Ch. 5) 5. Nonnormal distributions(Ch. 6) 6. Correlation Modeling (Ch. 7) 7. Term structure of risk (Ch. 8) 8. Copulas (Ch. 9) 9. Option Pricing (Ch. 10) 10. Option Risk Management (Ch. 11) 11. Credit Risk Management (Ch. 12)
learning_outcomes: In this course you will develop the following skills: 1. Apply statistics and mathematics to real financial problems 2. Read and write financial problems (from the formula on paper to Excel) 3. Problem solver and problem implementation: Learn advanced finance concepts and how to solve them in Excel 4. Become an expert of the equity market and its risk management tools
assessment_methods: Attendance (10%): based on class attendance Final Project (5%): to be done in groups of two Assignments (5%): to be done in groups of two. Midterm (30%): The examination is open book in Excel. Final Exam (50%): comprehensive exam of all material covered
literature: Elements of Financial Risk Management by Peter Christoffersen. Second Edition 2012. Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives by John Hull. Value at Risk by Philippe Jorion.

aims: This course is designed to be thorough introduction to derivatives products and markets. We will focus on describing the mechanics, pricing, hedging and applications of the basic linear (forwards, futures and swaps) and non-linear derivatives (options). This class will emphasize the relationship between non arbitrage and risk neutral valuation as the main cornerstone for derivative pricing. On a practical note, we will focus on the use of these instruments to transfer, mitigate, and eliminate different types on financial risk arising in industry situations. We will start by introducing the primary concepts in an intuitive manner and then, after formalizing these concepts in a more rigorous theoretical framework, we will follow with more complex examples and practical applications. We will also emphasize economic intuition, industry relevance and corporate applications whenever possible, especially in the second part of the course.
course_content: 1- Introduction: Definitions. Main types of Derivatives: Forwards, Futures and Options. Exchanges and OTC markets. Hedgers vs Speculators. The No Arbitrage principle. Margin trading and short-selling. 2- Forwards and Futures: Forward pricing using the non-arbitrage principle. Currency Forwards. Commodity Forwards. Lease Rate and Convenience Yield. Futures and its difference with Forwards. 3- Introduction to Risk Management: Hedging with Forwards and Futures. Pros and Cons of Hedging. Basis Risk. Minimum Variance Hedge. Options as Insurance. 4- Introduction to Fixed Income: Bond Basics. Spot/Zero and Forward Rates. Forward Rate Agreements. Eurodollar and Treasury Bond Futures. Duration and Convexity 5- Swaps: Interest Rate Swaps (IRS). Mechanics and uses. Valuation of Interest Rate Swaps. Currency Swaps. Total Return Swaps 6- Introduction to Options: Types of Options. Payouts and mechanics. Put Call Parity. Upper and Lower Bound on Option Prices. Basic option strategies (spreads, collars, straddles, strangles). Applications to Risk Management. 7- Introduction to Option Pricing: Basic replication argument. Two period binomial tree. Risk Neutral Probability. Multiperiod extensions and application to puts and American options 8- The Black Scholes Formula: Binomial Tree and Lognormality. Modeling Stock Returns. Basic Derivation of the Black Scholes Formula. Application to options on dividend paying assets and currency options. 9- Black Scholes Greeks: Delta, gamma, theta, vega and rho. Market making and delta hedging. Option Elasticity. 10- Implied Volatility: Volatility smile and volatility skew. Volatility Modeling and Estimation, ARCH and GARCH 11- Montecarlo Simulation: Simulating random walks and log normal stock prices. Montecarlo valuation of European and path dependent options. Introduction to Jumps. Estimation errors and Variance Reduction Techniques 12- Asset Pricing Theory: Non Arbitrage Revisited. Non Arbitrage, the Portfolio Choice Problem and Risk Neutral Valuation. Martingale Pricing. Revisiting the Black Scholes Formula using Risk Neutral Valuation 13- Corporate Applications Part I: Introduction to Real Options. Investment and NPV Rule. Investing as an Option. Practical Applications:. Investment or abandonment, R&D as an Option, Commodity Extraction. 14- Corporate Applications Part II: Debt and Equity as Options. Debt, credit risk, expected return and yield to maturity. Warrants and Convertibles Bonds. Stock Options. Modeling acquisitions with options. 15- Introduction to Credit Derivatives: Merton’s structural model. Default Probabilities, Exposure and Recovery. Survival, probabilities and hazard rates. Credit default swaps (CDS), mechanics and pricing.
learning_outcomes: By the end of the class, you should at least have a basic understanding of the workings and pricing of the main derivatives instruments and understand their usefulness and applications in a variety of industry settings. In addition, the class should provide a solid theoretical background for those who wish to take more advanced courses in derivatives, asset pricing, or other related topics. More specifically, you should be able to: Describe the mechanics, payouts, and applications of the most common derivatives instruments: forwards, futures, swaps and options in its many variations and forms. Apply non arbitrage arguments and risk neutral valuation to price these derivatives instruments across asset classes (equity, currencies, commodities, interest rate products, etc.). Develop some familiarity with numerical methods, primarily binomial trees and Montecarlo simulation, that can be used to value more complex derivatives for which an analytical solution might not exists. Recognize and understand the main corporate applications of derivatives products, such as real options, warrants and convertible debt issuance.
assessment_methods: The mid-term exam will be a 2.5-3 hour in-class exam, most likely a combination of a multiple choice section and a written section. You will be allowed to bring any summary sheet, class notes or academic reference. Any computing equipment can be used. The final exam will be a 3 hour exam on a date designated by the University. It will be on a similar format to the midterm (open book, part multiple choice, part written section). In addition, I will assign homework after each class. The homework won’t be graded but it is a critical part of the learning process as it serves a twofold purpose: it is meant to consolidate your understanding of the concepts imparted in class via additional practical exercises, and it will help you prepare for the midterm and final exams. The homework questions have a similar style to those on the exam, so I would strongly encourage you to solve all the exercises assigned in due course. Between class attendance, readings and homework assignments, I expect you to devote at least 10-12 hours a week to learn the class material. The grading will be based on the following formula: max (40% midterm+60% final, 50% midterm+50% final)
literature: Robert L McDonald, Derivatives Markets, International Edition, Pearson. John Hull: Fundamentals of Futures and Options Markets, Fourth Edition, Prentice Hall. David Luenberger, Investment Science, Oxford University Press, First Edition.

aims: The objective of this course is to undertake a rigorous study of various fixed income securities, including their derivatives and study how monetary policy affects the bond market. After a brief overview of the market for fixed income securities we will discuss basic pricing of various types of bonds, interest rate risk management of bonds and their portfolios. We will also analyze the green, social and sustainability fixed income market. Furthermore, we will encounter various types of interest rate derivatives and discuss their pricing. Additionally will also analyze the role of the central bank and inflation when pricing bonds.
course_content: 1. Overview of Fixed Income Markets 2. Basic Fixed Income Analysis Time Value of Money and Present Value Bond Pricing Yield-to-Maturity Extracting the Zero-Coupon Discount Curve Day Count Conventions Accrued Interest Floating Rate Bonds 3. Interest Rate Risk Management Key factors that affect the bond price Duration: A measure of interest rate sensitivity Convexity: Measuring the non-linear impact of interest rate changes Cash flow matching and dynamic immunization Asset - liability management Extension: Adding slope and curvature changes: The factor model Extension: Fixed income and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Hedging approaches using duration, convexity, slope and curvature 4. Green, Social and Sustainability Fixed Income Market 5. Interest Rate Derivatives Forwards Futures Swaps Options 6. Inflation, Monetary Policy and the Federal Funds Rate Central Banks: Mandate and Tools Monetary Policy: Implementation Understanding the Term Structure of Interest Rates Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities
